policy_statement: By 2025, assess nationwide climate change risk to vulnerable groups, focusing on indigenous peoples, poverty, and gender (A4c).
mechanism_description: Conduct nationwide assessments of climate change risks to vulnerable groups to inform adaptation planning and prioritization.
primary_causal_pathway: Risk/cost assessment → improved prioritization & planning → adaptation measures implemented → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ climate damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the governance/decision system by quantifying costs of inaction and past losses to inform plans, budgets, and priorities; (2) the mediating process is better risk recognition and economic appraisal that reallocates resources toward high-impact resilience actions and away from maladaptive choices; (3) the outcome is reduced climate risk (lower expected losses and damages from hazards such as floods, heat, drought) through better-designed and better-funded adaptation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved adaptation planning and investment decisions that reduce exposure and vulnerability, lowering future climate impact damages.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: information/economic assessment → improved planning decisions → implementation of resilience actions → reduced vulnerability and damages (adaptation outcome).  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while assessments may incidentally support mitigation prioritization, the mechanism as stated centers on climate impacts, losses, and the costs of inaction—i.e., managing climate risk rather than reducing emissions or improving resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: if such assessments are explicitly used to select emissions-reduction investments (e.g., social cost of carbon to drive decarbonization), the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; however, that would be a different canonical mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy framed as estimating costs of inaction/historic losses to inform planning is primarily enabling adaptation decision-making and thus fits Adaptation as the primary category.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: any finer tagging (e.g., “enabling governance,” “risk assessment,” “loss and damage analytics”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the cost assessment is explicitly tied to valuing avoided emissions (e.g., applying the social cost of carbon to prioritize mitigation measures), the dominant pathway may become Mitigation; otherwise, when focused on losses/damages and risk reduction, Adaptation is appropriate.
key_indicators: “By 2025”; “assessments of climate change risk”; “vulnerable groups nation-wide”; “special focus on indigenous peoples, poverty and gender”
co_benefits: Potential social equity and inclusion improvements in public planning (indigenous rights, poverty reduction targeting, gender mainstreaming), but not specified as outcomes beyond the risk assessment itself.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as information-disclosure if the policy explicitly required public reporting/publication of results; here it only specifies conducting “assessments,” so planning-strategy is the best fit.
